meta.title: brev

source.publication: Section 4: Roots, Prefixes, Suffixes and Foreign Words (owner-supplied reference)
source.usage_note: Personal study only. Root, words and meanings transcribed faithfully from the source PDF; a small number of well-attested real words on the same root are included where the source list was too short to form a complete family (4 to 8 members). Parts, glosses, context sentences, mentor note, attempt and construct options are the teaching layer, authored for the Language Garden (LANGUAGE_GARDEN_BIBLE §10).

meta.reviewer_notes: Batch 2: transcribed from the owner's roots (Greek and Latin) reference; 2 taught, 2 reach.

root.label: brev
root.mentor_note: brev is a small root that keeps its own promise: everything it builds stays short.

attempt.direction_prompt: A long, unabridged version, or a shortened one?

context_sentences: The report was so dense with abbreviations that it came with its own two-page glossary.
She never learned the abbreviation for the department’s own name until her third week there.
The editor prized brevity above almost everything else, cutting entire paragraphs down to a single line.
His toast lasted barely thirty seconds, and its brevity was exactly what the room needed that night.
The monk carried a worn breviary everywhere, its pages softened by decades of daily use.
A breviary of the committee’s three hundred page report circulated to the board within a day.

construct_options.text: A condensed summary of a longer text, especially of daily prayers
A long, unabridged history of a religious order
A collection of letters written between friends

context_sentences: The style guide insisted every state name be abbreviated the same way throughout the document.
She abbreviated the surgery’s risks so much in conversation that he agreed without understanding half of them.

construct_options.text: To shorten a word, phrase, or process
To lengthen or add unnecessary detail to something
To carve words permanently into a hard surface
